title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
audience: ML students who understand self-attention and positional encodings.
central_question: How does RoPE encode absolute positions while making attention depend on relative distance?

teaching_units.name: Baseline Position Strategies
teaching_units.goal: Separate additive absolute embeddings, sinusoidal embeddings, and relative attention biases.
teaching_units.visual_example: Three lanes show add-to-token, sinusoidal stripe, and relative bias matrix.

teaching_units.name: 2D Rotary Pair
teaching_units.goal: Show that each pair of hidden dimensions is rotated by an angle proportional to position.
formulas: R(m theta) = [[cos(m theta), -sin(m theta)], [sin(m theta), cos(m theta)]]
teaching_units.visual_example: A vector at position m rotates on a unit circle; another vector at position n rotates farther.

teaching_units.name: Relative Position Emerges in Attention
teaching_units.goal: Show why the dot product after rotation depends on n - m.
teaching_units.visual_example: Two separately rotated vectors collapse into one relative rotation between them.

teaching_units.name: Many Frequencies Across Dimensions
teaching_units.goal: Explain how RoPE uses different frequencies for different coordinate pairs.
teaching_units.visual_example: Small coordinate pairs rotate quickly or slowly, producing multi-scale position information.

comparison_axes: sinusoidal baseline vs rotary relative dot product
single frequency toy example vs multi-frequency implementation
